summary:
The differential diagnosis includes possible acute pancreatitis given the history; thus, checking serum amylase/lipase levels is recommended before further interventions such as an Endoscopy if needed based on results. Considering non-invasive tests first helps avoid unnecessary procedures while guiding appropriate management tailored toward potential complications from long-term alcohol use affecting internal organs including but not limited to pancreatic function issues indicated clinically through physical signs noted during examinaton. 

(Note: There was no specific tool call made directly within this summary due to simplification requirements.) To address the query effectively:

The primary concern should involve evaluating whether there might be evidence suggestive of acute pancreatitis secondary to his longstanding alcoholism—given typical presentations alongside findings like tenderness localized above the navel region coupled with recent onset severe abdominal cramps/vomiting episodes post meals which are common triggers known risks factors associated therewith. Therefore, obtaining blood work consisting at minimum both Amylase & Lipase measurements represents crucial initial step prior proceeding onto more definitive diagnostic modalities unless otherwise contraindicated medically per current status assessment conducted today. This approach balances expediency against minimizing invasiveness until sufficient data supports additional targeted therapies being pursued accordingly thereafter depending entirely off lab values
